meta.title: 76 Standard Solutions for Inventive Problems
meta.source: Adunka, R. (2016). 76 standard solutions: Creativity can be learned.

conventions.S1: The substance that needs to be changed, processed, transformed, detected, or controlled
conventions.S2: The substance that exerts a necessary effect as a tool (instrument or medium)
conventions.F: The field that generates the energy or force enabling interaction between S1 and S2

procedure: 1. Describe the problem to be solved
2. List all substances associated with the interactions of the problem
3. Create the SFM (Su-Field Model) of the engineering problem
4. Write down the standard solution that can be used to solve the problem
5. Write down the new SFM using the standard solutions
6. Describe the solution generated by implementing the standard solution

1.name: Build or destroy a Su-Field
1.tagline: Construction and decomposition of complete substance-field models
1.description: Creating or destroying the substance-field model if it is incomplete or has harmful functions.
1.application_guide: If the SFM model is not complete, apply standard 1.1.1 and class 5. If the SFM model shows harmful effects, apply group 1.2 and class 5.

1.1.name: Generation of substance-field models
1.1.description: Completing an incomplete Su-Field system so that two substances and one field are present.

solutions.name: Construction of a complete substance-field model
solutions.description: Complete the substance-field system so that two substances and one field (interaction) are present. A technical system is only complete and functional if it contains S1 (target object), S2 (tool), and F (interaction field).
solutions.mechanism: Identify the missing component (substance or field) and add it to construct a complete SFM.

solutions.name: Internal complex Su-Field with internal additives
solutions.description: Introduce an additive (S3) into the interior of one of the existing substances (S1 or S2) to improve controllability or desired properties. The original field remains unchanged. Examples: flux-core solder, doped silicon wafers, self-healing composites.
solutions.mechanism: Insert an additive within S1 or S2 to create a complex internal structure without changing the outer system or field.

solutions.name: External complex Su-Field with external additives
solutions.description: Attach an external additive (S3) outside S1 or S2 when internal modification is not possible. The additive must not introduce a new field. Examples: soapy water for leak detection, antiviral glove coating, ski wax.
solutions.mechanism: Add an external substance (S3) that supports the interaction without introducing new fields.

solutions.name: Using substances from the system environment
solutions.description: Use an already existing substance from the environment (S_EXT) as a functional component instead of adding new resources. Examples: air resistance as vibration amplifier, seawater as buoy stabilizer, self-sealing cable using moisture.
solutions.mechanism: Integrate an environmental substance (S_EXT) permanently or temporarily into the existing model.

solutions.name: Decomposition of the system environment and addition of additives
solutions.description: When the environment lacks usable substances, decompose, replace, or modify the environment to create functional additives. Three approaches: (1) replace environment with another, (2) decompose existing environment, (3) modify environment by adding an additive.
solutions.mechanism: Split, modify, or specifically use the system environment to crystallize a suitable additive (S'_EXT).

solutions.name: Optimal operation (minimum/maximum method)
solutions.description: Add a lot and remove the excess. When precise dosing is difficult, introduce an excess of field effect or substance quantity and subsequently remove it: (1) excess field is compensated by a new substance, (2) excess substance is removed by a field effect. Examples: painting by oversize and centrifugal force, overfilled mold straightened.
solutions.mechanism: Deliberately create an excessive effect and remove or compensate for the excess to achieve optimal system performance.

solutions.name: Maximum operation with intermediate transmitter
solutions.description: When maximum field effect is needed but direct application is not permissible, direct the maximum effect to a new transmitter substance (S_V) attached to the original substance. The transmitter filters, buffers, or transforms the field and passes only the desired portion to S1. Examples: laser cutting with focus lens, induction heating with ferrite core, capacitor for controlled discharge.
solutions.mechanism: Introduce a mediator substance (S_V) that receives the full field and transmits only the required portion to the target.

solutions.name: Selective maximum operation
solutions.description: Achieve selective, differentiated effect by using maximum field intensity only where needed. Two variants: (1) Use maximum field with protective substances where minimum effect is required. (2) Use minimal field with a local amplifying substance (S3) at the target site. Examples: laser cutting with protective coating, local catalyst for chemical activation, microwave heating with local metal particles.
solutions.mechanism: Create selective effect through targeted protection or local amplification, not through global field intensity.

variants.name: Selective maximum operation (maximum field)
variants.description: Use the field at maximum strength and protect areas not to be affected with a suitable protective substance or structure.

variants.name: Selective maximum operation (minimum field)
variants.description: Use a globally weak field (F_min) and apply an amplifying substance (S3) specifically at the target to locally strengthen the field effect.

1.2.name: Decomposition of substance-field models
1.2.description: Eliminating harmful effects in substance-field models by inserting third substances, modifying existing substances, redirecting fields, or introducing counter-fields.

solutions.name: Elimination of harmful effect by a foreign substance
solutions.description: Insert a third substance (S3) between S1 and S2 that blocks the harmful effect while passing through the beneficial effect. S3 should be as inexpensive and readily available as possible. Examples: surgical gloves, load-distributing steel plate, blast gas with coating.
solutions.mechanism: Insert a foreign substance between S1 and S2 to block the harm, not the benefit.

solutions.name: Elimination of harmful effect through system resources
solutions.description: Introduce a modified substance (S'_1,2) derived from S1 or S2 instead of an external foreign substance. Uses modifications like changes in aggregate state, shape, or geometry. Examples: stationary ball layer from same material to protect pipe walls, cold-treated tubes for form-fit assembly, flexible flaps in umbrella material.
solutions.mechanism: Transform or derive a modified substance from S1 or S2 that acts between them and reduces the harmful effect.

solutions.name: Redirection of harmful field effect (bodyguard principle)
solutions.description: Introduce an inexpensive, easily replaceable sacrificial substance (S2) to which the damaging energy is specifically redirected. Examples: elastic intermediate layer in freezing containers, cavities in ground to protect pipes from frost heave, lead apron for X-ray protection.
solutions.mechanism: Add a sacrificial substance that absorbs, deflects, or compensates for the harmful field effect.

solutions.name: Neutralization of harmful effect by a second field
solutions.description: Introduce a second field (F2) that counteracts the harmful effect or converts it into a beneficial effect, while F1 continues its positive function. No new substances are added and direct contact is maintained. Examples: electrostatic field to open flowers against air flow, anti-noise through phase shift, counter-sound for pump noise suppression.
solutions.mechanism: Add a second field F2 that neutralizes or compensates the negative effect of F1, creating a dual SFM.

solutions.name: Switching off magnetic fields and demagnetization
solutions.description: Eliminate undesired magnetic interactions by changing the magnetic properties of the substance through a second field: heating above Curie temperature, mechanical shocks, vibrations, or opposing fields (demagnetization). Examples: demagnetization of magnetic powder during welding, inductive heating with self-limiting Curie point, magnetic crane with passive hold via opposing field.
solutions.mechanism: Eliminate the magnetic property that causes the problem rather than mechanically shielding the field.

2.name: Improve the Su-Field
2.tagline: Improvement and further development of substance-field models
2.description: Introduce modifications within system boundaries to improve the efficiency and controllability of the technical system. Covers linked SFMs, double SFMs, segmentation, dynamization, rhythm coordination, porosity, and ferromagnetic substances.
2.application_guide: If the SFM is complete and has no harmful effects, but the effect is insufficient, apply class 2 or class 3 plus class 5.

2.1.name: Transition to complex substance-field models
2.1.description: Improving SFM efficiency through chaining or doubling of substance-field models.

solutions.name: Linked substance-field models (chain SFM)
solutions.description: Increase efficiency by creating a chain of SFMs: (a) replace one substance with an independent SFM triad, creating SFM1 -> SFM2 -> ..., or (b) insert a third substance S3 between S1 and S2 as an internal mediator. Examples: two-part wedge for easier disassembly, hammer-chisel-rock chain, AZIPOD ship drive with cascaded SFMs.
solutions.mechanism: Systematically extend the SFM by substitution or nesting to create a chained or stepped structure.

solutions.name: Formation of a double substance-field model (dual SFM)
solutions.description: Introduce a second, easily controllable field (F2) without changing the existing substances. F2 increases effectiveness, provides precise control, or produces complementary effects. Examples: ultrasonic-assisted electrolyte removal, electromagnetic rotation with hydrostatic pressure, bonding combined with spot welding.
solutions.mechanism: Bring a second field into play that helps, controls, or supplements the original field.

2.2.name: Further development of substance-field models
2.2.description: Improving SFM through better field control, fragmentation, porosity, dynamization, and structuring of fields and substances.

solutions.name: Application of fields with higher controllability
solutions.description: Replace an uncontrollable or difficult-to-control field (F1) with a more precisely controllable field (F_new), possibly with a new substance. Field hierarchy by controllability: gravitational -> mechanical -> thermal -> magnetic -> electric -> optical. Also optimize field waveform (stationary -> continuous -> pulsed/modulated) and direction. Examples: water jet instead of saw blade, inkjet with heat-controlled droplet size, electric control replacing hydraulic.
solutions.mechanism: Replace the field with one higher in the controllability hierarchy, and optimize waveform and direction of action.

solutions.name: Fragmentation of substances
solutions.description: Increase effectiveness by increasing the degree of fragmentation of the active substance along the progression: monolith -> segmented object -> powder -> liquid -> gas -> field (takes over the function). Examples: knife -> serrated blade -> micro-crusher -> abrasive -> laser; demountable cores in injection molding; distributed grid computing.
solutions.mechanism: Convert a monolithic substance into more fragmented forms for better adaptation, distribution, and controllability.

solutions.name: Transition to capillary and porous substances
solutions.description: Replace solid fabric with capillary-porous material following: solid -> with cavity -> perforated -> capillary-porous -> micro-capillary-porous. Pores can be filled with substances for special properties. Examples: capillary writing systems, aluminum sandwich with expanding powder, thermal paint with ceramic hollow spheres.
solutions.mechanism: Use cavities, pores and capillaries as effect enhancers for fine distribution, storage, insulation, dosing, or control.

solutions.name: Dynamization of substance-field models
solutions.description: Increase degrees of freedom and flexibility. For substances: add joints (linear, rotational), make connections flexible/spring-loaded, use flexible materials. For fields: stationary -> variable (pulsed, periodic, modulated). Examples: folding doors -> roller shutters -> bendable doors; pulsed water jet for better cleaning; adaptive mountain bike suspension.
solutions.mechanism: Increase the mobility and adaptability of the system to react efficiently to changing requirements.

solutions.name: Structuring of fields
solutions.description: Convert a uniform/disordered field into a non-uniform field with defined spatial or temporal structure. Spatial: standing waves, interference, polarization. Temporal: pulsed, periodic, modulated. Examples: structured magnetic field for powder mixing, ultrasonic wood welding, polarized light for contrast enhancement.
solutions.mechanism: Direct the field effect to where it is needed through spatial or temporal structuring.

solutions.name: Structuring of materials
solutions.description: Replace a substance with homogeneous/disordered structure by one with heterogeneous/ordered structure. Spatial structures: multilayered, fibrous, granular, honeycomb, crystalline, micro/nano. Temporal structures: adaptive, shape-memory, phase-reversing. Examples: pore structure via burned threads, nanostructured sensors, Colman lantern ceramic.
solutions.mechanism: Create internal order in the substance (physical, chemical, geometric, crystalline) for targeted effect and functionalization.

2.3.name: Coordination of rhythmics
2.3.description: Improving SFM through frequency matching, field rhythm synchronization, and temporal decoupling of incompatible effects.

solutions.name: Coordination of frequencies between field and substance
solutions.description: Tune the frequency of the acting field to the natural frequency of S1 or S2 (for resonance amplification) or deliberately deviate from it (to avoid damage). Consider fundamental frequency, harmonics, and anti-resonance conditions. Examples: resonance-adjusted arc welding, ultrasound against kidney stones, asymmetric gears to avoid resonance.
solutions.mechanism: Optimize or avoid resonance effects by tuning the field frequency to the natural frequency of a participating substance.

solutions.name: Tuning field rhythm in complex substance-field models
solutions.description: In systems using multiple fields simultaneously, tune (or deliberately detune) the frequencies and rhythms of the fields. Synchronization amplifies, desynchronization suppresses/decouples. Tuning parameters: frequency, phase, pulse duration, modulation, time offset. Examples: powder coating with coordinated electric and magnetic fields, Bluetooth frequency hopping, fly-fishing line control.
solutions.mechanism: Control interactions between multiple fields through synchronization or desynchronization of their rhythms.

solutions.name: Coordination of incompatible or independent effects
solutions.description: Execute incompatible effects by using the pauses of one effect to perform the other. Transition from continuous to periodic effects with coordinated frequencies. Examples: measurement during welding current pauses, GPRS data in GSM voice pauses, computer multitasking via time slicing.
solutions.mechanism: Temporally decouple mutually exclusive effects by using pauses and time multiplexing.

2.4.name: Complex ferromagnetic substance-field models
2.4.description: Improving SFM through ferromagnetic substances, particles, fluids, capillary-porous structures, control fields, dynamization, structuring, rhythm tuning, and electric/electrorheological alternatives.

solutions.name: Application of ferromagnetic substances and magnetic fields
solutions.description: Replace a non-magnetic substance with a non-fragmented ferromagnetic substance (Sm) that interacts with a magnetic field (F_mag) for contactless fixation, positioning, movement, or levitation. Examples: magnetic pipe alignment, magnetized screwdriver tip, magnetic component fixation.
solutions.mechanism: Use a compact ferromagnetic material with a magnetic field for contactless mechanical functions.

solutions.name: Application of ferromagnetic particles
solutions.description: Replace all or part of a ferromagnetic substance with ferromagnetic particles (S^mu_m) in combination with a magnetic field. Smaller particles -> better control (coarse grains -> fine grains -> powder). Examples: robot gripper with magnetic powder, pathogen detection with magnetic spheres, controllable rubber molds with magnetic granulate.
solutions.mechanism: Use crushed, finely dispersed ferromagnetic particles for adaptive, dynamic, or sensitive magnetic response.

solutions.name: Application of magnetic fluids (ferrofluids)
solutions.description: Replace ferromagnetic particles with a magnetic fluid (Smf) -- a colloidal suspension of very fine ferromagnetic particles in a carrier fluid. External magnetic fields control viscosity, density, adhesion, damping, or movement within milliseconds. Examples: magnetorheological shock absorbers, Curie-temperature door seals, rotating shaft seals.
solutions.mechanism: Use ferrofluids for fast, reversible, and dynamic control of material properties via magnetic fields.

solutions.name: Capillary and porous materials in Ferro-SFM
solutions.description: Combine capillary-porous structures (Scp) with magnetic substances for dual active structure: magnetic field controls movement, capillary structure distributes material. Examples: wave soldering with magnetic-porous cylinder, micro valves with ferrofluid in capillaries, dynamic filters with ferromagnetic elements.
solutions.mechanism: Synergize magnetic controllability with capillary effects for targeted fluid conduction, filtering, and dosing.

solutions.name: Complex ferromagnetic SFM with internal/external additives
solutions.description: When direct replacement by ferromagnetic particles is not permitted, add ferromagnetic substances internally (mixed in/stored) or externally (attached/coupled) to achieve magnetic controllability. Examples: non-magnetic objects transported in ferromagnetic powder, magnetizing coatings, magnetic carrier molecules for drug delivery.
solutions.mechanism: Add ferromagnetic elements to the existing system internally or externally without replacing the original substances.

solutions.name: Ferromagnetic SFM with system environment
solutions.description: When internal/external ferromagnetic additives are not permitted, introduce ferromagnetic particles into the system environment. The magnetic field changes environmental properties (viscosity, adhesion, movement) for indirect control. Examples: object sinking control in ferrofluid, magnetic carpet for assembly, surgical mat with magnetic structure.
solutions.mechanism: Modify the system environment with ferromagnetic particles so the system becomes indirectly controllable.

solutions.name: Additional control field in Ferro-SFM
solutions.description: Introduce an additional control field (Fc) that influences ferromagnetic properties through physical effects. Key effects: Curie point (loss of magnetism above Tc), Hopkins effect (increased magnetizability at 0.92-0.99 Tc), Barkhausen effect (abrupt magnetization changes). Examples: magnetic amplifier at near-Curie temperature, MRI imaging, superconducting shielding.
solutions.mechanism: Use a physically acting control field (thermal, electrical, frequency) to dynamically adjust magnetic material properties.

solutions.name: Dynamization of ferromagnetic SFM
solutions.description: Increase flexibility and adaptability of Ferro-SFM. Dynamize substances (joints, elasticity, inflatable structures) and fields (stationary -> pulsating, modulated magnetic fields). Examples: elastic magnetic measuring balloon for wall thickness, movable ferrofluid for adaptive valves, diaphragm movement measurement with magnetic coil.
solutions.mechanism: Make ferromagnetic systems more adaptive through movable, flexible structures and variable magnetic fields.

solutions.name: Structuring in ferromagnetic SFM
solutions.description: Convert uniform/unstructured magnetic fields and substances into non-uniform/structured ones. Spatial structuring: field line focus, interference, gratings. Temporal structuring: pulses, modulated sequences. Examples: fiber production by magnetic drawing, spherical electric motor with 3D field control, conductivity increase through aligned particles.
solutions.mechanism: Create locally differentiated effects through selective, asymmetric field and material structuring.

solutions.name: Rhythm tuning in Ferro-SFM
solutions.description: Tune the rhythms and natural frequencies of all Ferro-SFM components (Sm, F_mag, Fc, environment). Use resonance tuning (F_mag = f_nat), anti-resonance (F_mag != f_nat), harmonics, and dynamic adjustment. Examples: magnetic particle separation with synchronous vibration, electron spin resonance (ESR), microwave heating at 2.45 GHz, resonant magnetic actuators.
solutions.mechanism: Synchronize or deliberately desynchronize the frequencies of magnetic fields, substances, and control fields.

solutions.name: Electric substance-field models (E-SFM)
solutions.description: When magnetism is excluded, use an external electric or electromagnetic field (F_el) with existing electrical properties (charge, current, voltage) of S1/S2. Development stages: simple E-SFM -> complex E-SFM with environment -> dynamic E-SFM -> structured E-SFM -> rhythmic E-SFM. Examples: rock blasting by pulsed current, conductive bearing grease, electromagnets above Curie point.
solutions.mechanism: Use electrically generated fields and forces (Coulomb, induction, voltage fields) instead of magnetic means.

solutions.name: Application of electrorheological fluids
solutions.description: Replace a substance with an electrorheological fluid (S_rf) in combination with a controllable electric field (F_el). ER fluids change viscosity up to quasi-solidification under electric field, reversibly and in milliseconds. Examples: universal clamp with ER fluid, dynamic shock absorber with E-field control, precise positioning via viscous control, switchable fluid barrier seals.
solutions.mechanism: Control viscosity through electric field strength for reversible clamping, damping, and positioning without magnetic components.

3.name: Transition to super-system and sub-system
3.tagline: Transition to macro and micro levels
3.description: Develop solutions at the super-system level (bi- and polysystems) or at the sub-system level (micro level). Combines systems, differentiates functions, trims redundancy, redistributes contradictions, and transitions to the micro scale.
3.application_guide: If the SFM is complete and has no harmful effects but the effect is insufficient, apply class 3 or class 2 plus class 5.

3.1.name: Transition to bi- and polysystems
3.1.description: Improving system effectiveness by combining multiple systems into bi- or polysystems, expanding connections, differentiating functions, trimming redundancy, and redistributing contradictions.

solutions.name: Synthesis of bi- and polysystems
solutions.description: Combine the system with one or more similar systems to form a bi- or polysystem. Combine substances, fields, SF pairs, complete SFMs, or entire systems in space and time. Examples: spring assemblies, binary adhesives, multi-layer fabric cutting, micro-channel filters, LED clusters, multi-turbine power plants, multi-pole electrochemical processing.
solutions.mechanism: Duplicate or multiply system elements to achieve redundancy, parallelism, scaling, or new functions.

solutions.name: Expansion of connections in bi- and polysystems
solutions.description: Establish and expand connections, communication, and interaction between the parts of the bi- or polysystem. Connections can be rigid (fixed) or dynamic (flexible, mobile). Examples: three cranes with rigid triangular coupling, tailored blanks in car bodies, multi-chamber immersion body, flexible catamaran beams, Bluetooth car radio linking music and phone.
solutions.mechanism: Create coordinated interaction through mechanical, fluidic, electrical, logical, or informational coupling between subsystems.

solutions.name: Differentiation in bi- and polysystems
solutions.description: Deliberately increase differences in properties and functions of subsystems through: (1) parameter variation of similar components, (2) combining different subsystems with complementary properties, (3) combining systems with inverse/opposite characteristics. Examples: colored pens, multi-ink printers, stapler with remover, pencil with eraser, photochromic sunglasses.
solutions.mechanism: Create functional diversity through variation, complementarity, or contrast between subsystems.

solutions.name: Reduction (trimming) in expanded bi- and polysystems
solutions.description: Reduce redundant or superfluous parts and auxiliary components by combining several functions into one multifunctional system component. Results in a new monosystem with higher functional density, starting the next evolutionary loop. Examples: dual-color LED replacing two LEDs, multifunction display, combined screw (self-drilling, self-tapping with washer), all-in-one stereo, integrated camera, automotive cockpit.
solutions.mechanism: Trim redundancy by folding multiple functions into a single multifunctional element, creating a higher-level monosystem.

solutions.name: Redistribution of incompatible properties
solutions.description: Distribute opposing, incompatible properties to the overall system and its parts using a two-level structure: the whole system has property A, its parts have property Anti-A. Examples: vice with flexible mounting (rigid grippers, flexible system), CFRP (individual layers compensate, overall structure stable), bicycle chain (rigid links, flexible chain), butcher's protective glove (micro-rigid, macro-flexible).
solutions.mechanism: Resolve physical contradictions by separating opposite properties between system level and part level.

3.2.name: Transition from macro to micro level
3.2.description: Increasing effectiveness by moving functions and system components to the microscopic level.

solutions.name: Transition from macro to micro level
solutions.description: Increase system effectiveness by transitioning to the micro level through: (1) performing functions at lower system levels, (2) using field interactions with microstructures (crystal lattice, molecules, atoms, nanoparticles), or (3) miniaturizing system components. Examples: thermal micro drive, microencapsulation, van der Waals bonding, nanomaterials, CD to MP3, inkjet printer, Pilkington glass process.
solutions.mechanism: Shift functions from the macroscopic to microscopic level using fields, nanostructures, or miniaturization for precision, new effects, and material efficiency.

4.name: Detect and measure
4.description: Solve measurement and detection problems of a technical system. Covers bypassing measurement, using copies, successive localization, building measurement SFMs, using system resonance, ferromagnetic measurement, and evolving measurement systems.
4.application_guide: If the problem is a measurement problem, apply class 4 and additionally class 5.

4.1.name: Indirect methods
4.1.description: Bypassing or simplifying measurement through system changes, copies, or successive localizations.

solutions.name: Bypassing measurement by changing the system
solutions.description: Redesign the system so that measurement, control, or detection becomes unnecessary because the desired function is performed automatically and intrinsically. Use materials with defined physical reactions (bimetal, Curie point), geometric self-limiting, or coupling of cause and effect. Examples: motor with Curie stop, bimetal switch, inductive heat treatment with salt melting indicator.
solutions.mechanism: Make the system self-regulating or self-indicating so that explicit measurement is no longer needed.

solutions.name: Use of copies for measurement
solutions.description: Perform measurement on a copy rather than the original. Copies can be models, images (visible, IR, UV, X-ray), shadows (light, heat, radio), impressions, scans, or virtual copies (CAD, FEM). Examples: snake length from photo with scale, 3D model from water lines, satellite troop count, negative overlay for perforated plate inspection, thermography.
solutions.mechanism: Measure a copy or image of the object to avoid danger, reduce costs, or handle inaccessible originals.

solutions.name: Replacing measurement with successive localizations
solutions.description: Transform a measurement problem into a sequence of simple binary state detections (threshold checks) instead of continuous measurement. Use indicators: phosphors, thermochromic/photochromic substances, radioactive tracers. Examples: thermochromic color for temperature threshold, Curie point marker in lubricant, ABS sensors for tire pressure loss detection.
solutions.mechanism: Replace continuous precise measurement with observing whether a threshold has been crossed or a state has changed.

4.2.name: Synthesis of measurement substance-field models
4.2.description: Building and extending measurement SFMs with additives, environmental resources, and field transformations.

solutions.name: Structure of a measurement SFM
solutions.description: Supplement the system by adding an input field F' so that the output field change F'' provides measurable information about the system state. Structure: F' -> S -> F''. Different field types can serve as input/output (e.g., mechanical -> electrical, optical -> thermal). Examples: boiling detection via resistance change, foil thickness via capacitance, belt tension via frequency change.
solutions.mechanism: Add a defined input field that generates a measurable output field change indicating the internal system state.

solutions.name: Complex measurement SFM with additives
solutions.description: Add an internal or external additive (S3) to S1 or S2 that can be easily detected with field F1 or provides a measurable signal F2. Structure: F1 -> S3 -> F2 (often invisible -> visible or not measurable -> measurable). Examples: fluorescent coolant leak detection, banknote UV inspection, DNA marker in car paint.
solutions.mechanism: Introduce a detectable marker substance that generates a measurable reaction indicating the system state.

solutions.name: Complex measurement SFM with system environment
solutions.description: When internal/external additives to the system are not allowed, add an additive S3 to the system environment (S_ext) that generates a measurable signal. The system state is derived from environmental changes. Examples: wear detection via fluorescent powder in lubricating oil, GPS position determination using satellite information field.
solutions.mechanism: Extend the system environment with a detectable additive and derive system properties from environmental state changes.

solutions.name: Utilization of resources in the system environment
solutions.description: When no additives can be introduced anywhere, obtain measurable substances by decomposing, changing aggregate state, or modifying the system environment itself. Use electrolysis, cavitation, or other effects to generate detectable markers from existing environmental resources. Examples: flow velocity via cavitation bubbles, subatomic particle detection in bubble/cloud chambers.
solutions.mechanism: Generate measurable substances from the environment itself by splitting, converting, or mobilizing existing resources.

4.3.name: Increasing effectiveness of measurement SFM
4.3.description: Enhancing measurement through effect databases, system resonance, and environmental resonance.

solutions.name: Application of the effect database
solutions.description: Define input and output fields (F1 -> F2) in the measurement SFM and search for suitable physical, chemical, or technical effects that enable or reinforce field transformation. Use effect databases. Example field pairs: electric->thermal (pyroelectric), thermal->optical (thermomechanical+mechano-optical), magnetic->electrical (Hall effect), chemical->optical (luminescence).
solutions.mechanism: Select known physical/chemical effects to transform an input field into an easily measurable output field.

solutions.name: Application of system resonance for measurement
solutions.description: When indicator additives and external fields are not permitted on the system, bring the system or its parts into resonance oscillation. Derive system state information from changes in oscillation frequency. Consider harmonics, damping, and aperiodicities. Examples: mass determination via resonance, tension measurement of belts/cables via frequency, FEM frequency response analysis.
solutions.mechanism: Use natural or resonance frequencies of the system to indirectly infer system state without additives or direct field influence.

solutions.name: Application of environmental resonance for measurement
solutions.description: When vibration excitation within the system is not permissible, set the system environment or connected objects into resonance vibration. Derive system information from changes in environmental oscillation frequency. Examples: boiling detection via vapor vibration, quartz crystal temperature measurement, capacitance measurement via resonant circuit, level measurement via wall resonance.
solutions.mechanism: Make the environment (not the system) resonate and derive system state from environmental resonance changes.

4.4.name: Electromagnetic measurement substance-field models
4.4.description: Ferromagnetic and electromagnetic measurement solutions for non-contact precision measurement.

solutions.name: Ferromagnetic measurement SFM
solutions.description: Use non-fragmented ferromagnetic substances (Sm) with a magnetic field (F_mag) instead of non-magnetic substances for precise, non-contact measurement of position, movement, force, or presence. Examples: bicycle brake light via magnet+Hall sensor, traffic light control via magnetic loops under pavement, ship hull inspection via embedded magnets and magnetometer.
solutions.mechanism: Detect system state through ferromagnetic material interaction with magnetic fields using Hall sensors, magnetometers, or reed contacts.

solutions.name: Measurement SFM with ferromagnetic particles
solutions.description: Replace solid ferromagnetic material with ferromagnetic particles (S^mu_m) for finer, more sensitive measurements. Changes in the system (curing, stress, density) change magnetic properties measured via external field. Examples: curing control via magnetic powder in resin, magnetic particle imaging (MPI) for blood flow, magnetoelastic composite sensors for strain.
solutions.mechanism: Use finely dispersed ferromagnetic particles for distributed, high-sensitivity measurement throughout the volume.

solutions.name: Complex ferromagnetic measurement SFM
solutions.description: When direct replacement with ferromagnetic particles is not permitted, construct a complex internal or external measurement SFM by adding ferromagnetic substances (Sm) permanently or temporarily as a supplement rather than substitution. Examples: polymerization control via temporarily introduced magnetic powder, rock breaking with ferrofluid pressure control.
solutions.mechanism: Add ferromagnetic substances as a supplementary subsystem without replacing original system components.

solutions.name: Complex ferromagnetic measurement SFM with environment
solutions.description: When no ferromagnetic particles may be added internally/externally, add ferromagnetic particles (S^mu_m) permanently or temporarily to the system environment (S_ext). Measure indirectly via environmental behavior in a magnetic field. Example: model ship wave analysis using ferromagnetic particles in water.
solutions.mechanism: Make the system environment the measurement medium by adding ferromagnetic particles to it.

solutions.name: Physical effects in magnetic measurement SFM
solutions.description: Utilize special electrical/electromagnetic/magnetic effects or introduce an additional control field (Fc) to influence ferromagnetic properties for measurement. Key effects: Barkhausen effect (magnetic noise for crack testing), Hopkins effect (sensitivity near Curie point), magnetostriction, magnetic resonance. Examples: crack testing of steel shafts via Barkhausen noise, magnetic amplifiers with temperature control.
solutions.mechanism: Use physical effects (Curie, Hopkins, Barkhausen) and control fields to create highly sensitive, selectively controllable measurement.

4.5.name: Evolution lines of measurement SFM
4.5.description: Evolving measurement systems through combination into bi-/poly-systems and differentiation of measurement functions.

solutions.name: Transition to bi- and poly-measurement systems
solutions.description: Combine the measurement system with one or more similar systems to form a bi- or poly-system. Combine substances, fields, SF pairs, or complete SFMs. Enables redundancy, distributed measurement, or functional combination. Examples: collective temperature measurement of multiple small objects, pyrometer with LED pilot light, optician's multi-device eye measurement.
solutions.mechanism: Use parallel or combined measurement systems for redundancy, robustness, multidimensional data, or complementary sensing.

solutions.name: Differentiation of measurement functions (derivatives)
solutions.description: Extend measurement to temporal or functional derivatives: 0th order (direct parameter), 1st order (change/gradient), 2nd order (rate of change of change). Provides deeper understanding of dynamic processes. Examples: seismic analysis (resistance -> resistance change -> activity), radar (position -> velocity -> acceleration), astrophysics redshift (speed -> acceleration of universe).
solutions.mechanism: Measure not just the static value but also its rate of change and acceleration for richer dynamic information.

5.name: Apply standards
5.tagline: Aids and standards for the application of standard solutions
5.description: Develop solutions that meet the requirements of an ideal system. Support the application of the first four classes through indirect methods for adding substances and fields, phase transitions, self-controlling transformations, field amplification, and particle generation.
5.application_guide: Apply class 5 in addition to whichever other class is relevant. When a proposed solution from classes 1-4 cannot be implemented directly, class 5 provides guidelines for realizing the solution indirectly.

5.1.name: Adding substances indirectly
5.1.description: Methods for introducing substances when direct addition is not permitted, including indirect techniques, substance splitting, self-elimination, and large-volume substitution.

solutions.name: Indirect processes for introducing substances
solutions.description: Nine indirect methods for introducing a substance when direct addition is not permitted. Includes: creating voids/empty spaces, using fields instead of substances, external instead of internal addition, very small amounts of highly active additives, locally concentrated addition, temporary introduction, use of copies, chemical compound decomposition, and generating substances from system/environment decomposition.
solutions.mechanism: Use indirect strategies when direct substance addition is prohibited for regulatory, safety, or functional reasons.

variants.name: Insert blank space (void instead of substance)
variants.description: Introduce a void, cavity, gas bubble, vacuum, or removable medium that performs the functional role of a material without being a permanent substance. Create via: removed carrier material (etched wires), cavitation, evaporation, sublimation, phase transitions.

variants.name: Field instead of substance
variants.description: Use a physical field (electric, magnetic, acoustic, pressure, temperature, radiation) as a functional substitute for a substance. Examples: thread deformation measurement via electric field, plastic container leak test in vacuum.

variants.name: External instead of internal addition
variants.description: Apply an additive to the system from the outside without penetrating the system interior. Examples: conductive liquid for wall thickness measurement, soapy water for external leak test, Teflon tape for thread sealing.

variants.name: Small amount of very active additive
variants.description: Use a minimal dose of a highly effective substance (catalysts, nanoparticles, enzymes, surfactants) that has a disproportionate effect. Examples: polymer addition to lubricating oil for pressure reduction, trace inhibitors for corrosion protection, catalyst-initiated reactions.

variants.name: Locally concentrated addition
variants.description: Apply the additive only at strategically selected points (surfaces, lines, points) where it is needed, using minimal total substance. Examples: magnetic-field-aligned conductive particles in plastic, laser-absorbing coating only at impact zone, corrosion protection at vulnerable points.

variants.name: Temporary introduction of additive
variants.description: Introduce the substance temporarily to achieve a short-term effect and then remove it without leaving residue (by dissolution, evaporation, melting, or extraction). Examples: ferromagnetic powder for temporary positioning, wax pattern for casting, short-term insulation coating.

variants.name: Insertion into a copy of the system
variants.description: Use a functional or structural copy of the system (physical model, virtual twin, scale model) into which the substance can be freely introduced. Examples: transparent model with liquid for stereometry measurement, flow simulation in transparent channel model, drug testing on cell cultures.

variants.name: Chemical compound decomposition to generate additives
variants.description: Introduce a reactive chemical precursor that decomposes in situ under specific conditions (thermolysis, hydrolysis, photolysis, mechanical release) to generate the needed additives. Examples: salt-impregnated wood releasing ammonia under friction, encapsulated additives for corrosion protection.

variants.name: Disassembly of system parts or environment
variants.description: Create the needed substance by decomposing the external environment or the system itself via electrolysis, cavitation, phase transition, or mechanical decomposition. Examples: electrolysis gas bubbles for flow mixing, temperature-induced vapor from ambient moisture, evaporation cooling.

solutions.name: Splitting of substances
solutions.description: Produce required new substances or system parts by splitting one of the existing substances into subcomponents, molecular groups, or functional units. Mechanisms: ionization, mechanical separation, phase separation, chemical decomposition, thermal decomposition, field-induced fragmentation. Examples: flow splitting for intensified mixing, gas ionization for conductivity, split propellers for supersonic noise avoidance.
solutions.mechanism: Decompose an existing substance chemically, physically, or mechanically to create needed functional components.

solutions.name: Self-elimination of substances
solutions.description: Introduce a substance that performs its function and then autonomously disappears through decomposition, transformation, evaporation, or integration into the existing system or environment. Mechanisms: evaporation/sublimation, thermal decomposition, chemical transformation, biological degradation, integration into base material. Examples: dry ice blasting, absorbable sutures, aluminum electrodes becoming part of Al2O3 product, fusible interfacing.
solutions.mechanism: Use substances that self-eliminate after performing their function, leaving no residue or becoming part of the system.

solutions.name: Introducing large quantities of material via empty space
solutions.description: When a large quantity of substance is needed but not permitted, use empty space in the form of inflatable structures, foam, or cellular structures to achieve the volume/surface effect with minimal material. Examples: inflatable balloons for aircraft recovery, foam insulation in immersion suits, inflatable guest mattresses, honeycomb sandwich structures.
solutions.mechanism: Create large-volume but lightweight structures using gas fillings, foams, and cellular materials instead of solid mass.

5.2.name: Adding fields
5.2.description: Methods for introducing fields by using existing system fields, environmental fields, or field-generating substances.

solutions.name: Multiple use of available fields
solutions.description: Identify and functionally expand system-internal fields so that one field fulfills multiple functions. Field types: mechanical, thermal, magnetic, electrical, optical, acoustic, chemical, biological, gravitational. Examples: speed sensor with self-energy generation via induction, self-inflating sleeping pad using foam restoring force, HF welding converting EM field to local heat, cyclotron Cherenkov radiation as measurable side effect.
solutions.mechanism: Redirect, amplify, modulate, or convert existing fields to fulfill new functions without adding components.

solutions.name: Adding available fields from the system environment
solutions.description: When internal fields are unavailable, use fields from the system environment: wind, water flow, gravity, solar radiation, temperature gradients, Earth's magnetic field, ambient noise, GPS, radio networks. Examples: river flow ejector suction, eCall using GPS/mobile networks, electronics cooling via waste heat convection, trim tabs using fluid pressure.
solutions.mechanism: Integrate external environmental fields as functional field sources without adding components to the system.

solutions.name: Use of field-generating substances
solutions.description: Use or modify existing substances to generate fields through their physical properties. Substance->field mappings: ferromagnetic->magnetic, thermoactive->temperature, radioactive->radiation, phosphorescent->optical, piezoelectric->electric, reactive->chemical/thermal. Examples: tool-workpiece thermocouple, phosphor UV->visible light conversion, radioactive beads for tumor treatment, coolant waste heat for cabin heating.
solutions.mechanism: Activate latent physical properties of existing materials to generate needed fields without external field sources.

5.3.name: Phase transitions
5.3.description: Utilizing changes of physical state and their accompanying phenomena for improved system functionality.

solutions.name: Change of physical state
solutions.description: Activate new or improved functions by changing the aggregate state of existing substances. Transitions: solid->liquid (coating, bonding), liquid->gaseous (cooling, pressure), solid->gaseous (cleaning, residue-free), solid<->crystal form (property change). Examples: ice blasting for cleaning, sugar crystals for temporary wound treatment, alpha/beta brass property change via temperature.
solutions.mechanism: Exploit the different properties (density, mobility, reactivity) of each aggregate state for new functions.

solutions.name: Reversible phase transitions (dual-state substances)
solutions.description: Use substances that can repeatedly and controllably assume different states depending on environmental conditions. Transitions: solid<->liquid (latent heat storage), solid<->solid crystal (shape memory), gas<->liquid (climate control), thermomechanical (bimetals). Examples: kerosene microcapsules for temperature regulation, shape memory alloy cooling plates, ice skate friction reduction, blow-molded plastic orientation.
solutions.mechanism: Use reversible phase changes for heat storage, control, regulation, energy buffering, and adaptive functions.

solutions.name: Application of accompanying phenomena of phase transitions
solutions.description: Use secondary physical effects that accompany phase transitions: energy absorption/release (cooling, heating), volume change (sealing, clamping), structural change (memory effect, stiffening), density change (separation, buoyancy), conductivity change (switching, insulation). Examples: zeolite cooling via evaporation/condensation, Wood's metal expansion for hermetic seals, hand warmer via exothermic crystallization.
solutions.mechanism: Harness side effects of phase transitions (volume, energy, structural, conductivity changes) for technical functions.

solutions.name: Two-phase systems
solutions.description: Replace single-phase substances with bi-phase combinations to combine advantages of both phases. Types: solid-liquid (abrasive pastes), liquid-gas (foams, aerosols), solid-gas (porous solids with gas filling), crystal-amorphous (phase change materials). Examples: polishing agent with abrasive particles in liquid, foam for emission reduction during cutting, air bubbles for diver training impact reduction.
solutions.mechanism: Combine two states of aggregation in one component to synergize flow, mechanical, thermal, or structural properties.

solutions.name: Interaction between phases
solutions.description: Introduce or intensify physical/chemical interaction between phases in two-phase systems for mass/energy transfer, catalysis, adsorption/desorption, selective permeability, or reaction. Examples: hydrogen storage via platinum/palladium metal absorbers, barrel maturation of spirits (liquid-wood interaction), zeolite cooling cycle via water vapor adsorption/desorption.
solutions.mechanism: Make the phase boundary an active functional unit through adsorption, reaction, bonding, or exchange.

5.4.name: Characteristics of physical effects
5.4.description: Self-controlling transformations and field amplification through critical-state triggering.

solutions.name: Self-controlling transformation
solutions.description: Use materials that autonomously switch between two states based on environmental parameters (temperature, pressure, light, fields) without external control or sensors. Mechanisms: aggregate state change, second-order phase transitions, ionization/recombination, shape memory, conductivity switching. Examples: ionizable gas in lightning rods, photochromic lenses (Photogray), shape memory alloys (NiTi) for thermal actuators.
solutions.mechanism: Use materials with dynamic, reversible properties that adapt automatically to changing environmental conditions.

solutions.name: Amplification of the output field (triggered critical state)
solutions.description: Store energy in the system near a critical threshold, then use a minimal trigger signal to produce a disproportionately strong effect. Mechanisms: superheated liquid (bubble chamber), explosives (chain reaction), resonance systems, catalytic processes, phase transitions. Examples: bubble chamber for particle detection, shaped charge explosives, shape memory metal shock generation, transistor current amplification.
solutions.mechanism: Keep the transforming material near a critical state so a weak input trigger releases stored energy as a strong output.

5.5.name: Generation of material particles and decomposition
5.5.description: Creating needed substance particles through decomposition of complex substances or synthesis from simpler ones, with strategic structural level selection.

solutions.name: Generation of substance particles by decomposition
solutions.description: Obtain required particles (ions, atoms, radicals) by decomposing a structurally more complex substance when direct availability or storage is problematic. Methods: electrolysis, thermal decomposition, photochemical decomposition, catalytic reaction, nuclear processes, plasma technology. Examples: hydrogen from salt water electrolysis, water splitting for O2/H2, UV decomposition of ozone for atomic oxygen.
solutions.mechanism: Break down a stable, storable substance to release reactive particles on demand at the point of use.

solutions.name: Generation of particles by synthesis
solutions.description: Construct required substances from structurally lower particles (ions, simple molecules, atoms) through controlled bottom-up assembly. Methods: chemical synthesis, biological synthesis (photosynthesis, fermentation), electrochemical processes, thermonuclear fusion, crystallization/cluster formation, enzymatic catalysis. Examples: water molecule clusters via EM field, aspirin synthesis, natural photosynthesis.
solutions.mechanism: Build more complex substances from simpler building blocks through controlled synthesis processes.

solutions.name: Characteristics of particle generation (structural level selection)
solutions.description: For decomposition: start from the next higher structural level (e.g., molecule -> ion, not macromolecule -> ion). For synthesis: start from the next lower structural level (e.g., ion -> molecule, not atom -> molecule). Structural levels: macrosystem -> molecules -> ions/atoms -> subatomic particles. Examples: water electrolysis (molecule->ions), ion recombination (ions->molecule), gas discharge ionization/recombination.
solutions.mechanism: Select the optimal starting structural level -- the next higher for decomposition, the next lower for synthesis -- to minimize energy and steps.
